query: location of olfactory receptors in nose

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Structure and Function -- Olfactory Region
retrieved_docs.content: Olfaction requires orthonasal or retronasal airflow to transport odor-bearing particles up to the olfactory epithelium located at the apex of the nasal cavity. As odorants become trapped in the mucus, it binds to odorant binding proteins that concentrate and help solubilize the particles. The particles are then attached to olfactory receptors on cilia that transmit specific signals up through the cribriform plate to synapse with neurons of the olfactory bulb, which then sends signals through the olfactory nerve (CNI) into the secondary neurons for higher processing before entering the brain. A unique feature of the olfactory receptors is that a single receptor cell can detect only one odorant type and cannot regenerate. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Olfactory -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The olfactory system is at the roof of the nasal cavity at the cribriform plate - a perforated portion of the ethmoid bone separating the frontal lobe of the cerebrum from the nasal cavity. Odorant molecules within the nasal passages first encounter receptors on the primary cilia of olfactory sensory neurons. Each neuron expresses a single type of protein receptor on these dendritic extensions. However, individual odorants can bind to many different receptor proteins. The dendritic ends of these first-order neurons are within a thin layer of mucus with adjacent supporting epithelium. Bowman glands secrete serous fluid rich in glycoprotein, which warms, moistens, and traps air, helping dissolve gaseous odorant particles.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nasal -- Cellular Level -- Neurons
retrieved_docs.content: Receptor neurons in the roof of the nasal cavity recognize and bind odor molecules. These stimuli lead to depolarization of the neurons and ultimately, signal propagation along the olfactory nerves toward the central nervous system (CNS).

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 1 (Olfactory) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Architecturally, the nose is designed in a way to facilitate the movement of inspired air toward the olfactory epithelium. The olfactory epithelium is specialized epithelial tissue covering the septum, the upper portion of the superior turbinate and the lateral surface of the posterosuperior portions of both nasal cavities. This specialized epithelium contains the cell bodies of bipolar olfactory neurons from which the olfactory nerve fibers originate and extend apically. In each nostril, human beings have 6 to 10 million olfactory sensory neurons distributed along a surface area of 2.5 cm^2. These cells continuously regenerate from stem cells within the basal portion of the epithelium and have a half-life of 30 to 40 days. The apical portion of these cells has dendrites that project into the epithelial surface, where they interact with odoriferous particles from the outside world via G protein-coupled receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nasal -- Function -- Olfaction
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, the nasal cavity enables olfaction. Olfaction helps to identify sources of nearby danger or nutrition, as well as influencing mood and sexuality. [11] As air enters the nasal cavity, the turbinates function to direct a portion of the airflow to the higher regions of the cavity. The olfactory cleft is at the roof of the nasal cavity near the cribriform plate. Olfactory receptors located here bind to odorants carried into the nose during inhalation and send signals to the olfactory cortex and other brain regions. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Olfactory Training -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The olfactory system is the most primitive of the human special senses and is very closely connected to the limbic system, the seat of emotion. [16] Airborne particulates are inhaled into the nose and dissolved in the mucinous secretions of the nasal cavity; ciliary action brings them into contact with the olfactory epithelium, where water-soluble molecules from these particulates bind to chemoreceptors that subsequently transduce signals via the unmyelinated axons of the olfactory projections up through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to the olfactory bulbs, and then ultimately to the olfactory cortex of the forebrain. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 0 (Terminal Nerve) -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Although CN0 is closely positioned next to the CNI (olfactory nerve), its functionality is distinct from the classical olfactory sensory role of perceiving odorant molecules via the olfactory receptors (ORs), which belong to a superfamily of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). [15] Various reports indicate that the terminal nerve has projections to several important neuroanatomical structures, such as the medial pre-commissural septum, including the medial septal nucleus. It also sends fibers to the nasal mucosa and rostral ventral brain structures, primarily olfactory and limbic areas (i.e., amygdala, hypothalamic nuclei). [16] [17] These connections provide access to the limbic system structures, such as the hypothalamus. In the hypothalamus, specifically within the preoptic (POA) and the infundibular (INF) nuclei, there is a relatively inconspicuous group of neurons collectively referred to as the "kisspeptin neuronal network" (KP). [18] This hypothalamic neuronal circuit is involved in the central endocrinologic control of puberty and human reproductive functions. The hypothalamic KP neurons act primarily by inducing GnRH secretion from the hypothalamus, which, in turn, regulates the secretion of the gonadotropin hormones LH and FSH. These adenohypophyseal hormones will ultimately influence the synthesis and release of sex steroids from the gonads. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Anosmia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Smell accounts for 95% to 99% of chemosensation; while, taste accounts for the rest of chemosensation. Anosmia is the inability to perceive smell/odor. It can be temporary or permanent and acquired or congenital. There are many causes. For example, any mechanical blockage preventing odors from reaching the olfactory nerves can cause a loss of sense of smell. This blockage can be due to inflammatory processes like simple infections causing mucus plugs or nasal polyps. Neurological causes can include disturbances to the sensory nerves that make up the olfactory bulb or anywhere along the path in which the signal of smell is transferred to the brain. To better understand this process, it is helpful to understand how people can perceive smell. When a particle with odorant molecules in the air is present, it travels up through the nasal canals to the nasal cavity, where olfactory receptor neurons extend from the olfactory bulb that sits on the cribriform plate of the brain. Each nasal cavity contains about 5 million receptor cells or neurons. There are 500 to 1000 different odor-binding proteins on the surface of these olfactory receptor cells. Each olfactory receptor cell expresses only one type of binding protein. These afferent olfactory neurons (cranial nerve I) facilitates the transfer of a chemical signal (particles in the air) to an electrical signal (sensed by afferent receptor neurons) which is then transferred and ultimately perceived by the brain. From the olfactory bulb, the signal is further processed by several other structures of the brain, including the piriform cortex, entorhinal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus. Any blockage or destruction of the pathway along which smell is transferred and processed may result in anosmia. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Phantosmia -- Introduction -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The olfactory epithelium is a pseudostratified columnar neuroepithelium at the superior nasal vault, between the septum and middle turbinate. [1] The basal cell layer of the olfactory epithelium contains stem cells, serving as the basis for its regenerative nature. Superficial to the basal layer lie olfactory receptor neurons, which transmit odorant signals through the olfactory nerve with or without inputs from the trigeminal nerve to the central nervous system, lending to odor perception. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 1 (Olfactory) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: After olfactory information is transmitted from olfactory receptor neurons to mitral and tufted cells in the glomeruli, the axonal projections of mitral and tufted cells form bundles that pass through the olfactory bulb and run dorsally, merging to form the olfactory tract. From the olfactory bulb, each olfactory tract runs posteriorly along the olfactory sulcus and ends in the olfactory trigone. The olfactory trigone is a triangular widening of the terminal olfactory tract located superior to the anterior clinoid process and directly rostral to the anterior perforated substance. At this unique anatomic landmark, the fibers of the tract diverge to form two main bundles, the lateral and medial olfactory stria.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Concha -- Nerves -- Somatosensory Innervation
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond autonomic innervation, somatic and sensory functions in the turbinates are critical and are carried out by the olfactory neuroepithelium and trigeminal nerve. [14] [15] The olfactory neuroepithelium is located in the superior, supreme, and middle turbinates, allowing dissolved particulate odorants to bind with olfactory receptors. The signals are transmitted through the cribriform plate of the skull base via the olfactory projections of the 1st cranial nerve to the olfactory cortex of the ventral forebrain, contributing significantly to the human sense of taste.

retrieved_docs.title: Phantosmia -- Introduction -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Each olfactory neuron expresses a specific G-protein coupled receptor, which, when bound by odorants, leads to signal transmission through the cribriform plate to the olfactory bulb. [1] Within the olfactory bulb, olfactory nerves of similar converge onto specific glomeruli to synapse with second-order neurons. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 0 (Terminal Nerve) -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, during the last several decades, the idea of pheromones influencing human sexual behaviors has been a controversial topic. [21] [22] This naturally includes the even more controversial vomeronasal organ (VNO), a very discrete chemosensory organ located deep into the nasal cavity of vertebrates, including humans. [23] [24] [25] For many years, the VNO has been held responsible for intra-specific chemical communication via pheromones, which are chemical messengers secreted externally by an individual and detected by another of the same species. Many still debate the existence of the VNO and pheromones in humans. However, the evidence coming from many reputable studies is robustly supportive. [26] These studies suggest that humans have the genes to produce at least six of the same pheromone receptors present in mice. The presence of VNO in humans has been massively described in the literature as well. The VNO is present in human fetuses by weeks 6 to 7; its ducts have openings into the nasal cavity by the 28th week. Histologically, it consists of the olfactory epithelium, lamina propria, and a rich vascularization network. A recent Bulgarian study reported the presence of the VNO in approximately 27% (males, 53%; females, 47%) of the adult population. [27] Similar findings have been reported in other populations (e.g., the United States, Canada, France, and Egypt).

retrieved_docs.title: Olfactory Training -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The olfactory epithelium is located at the apex of the nasal cavity, along both the upper septum and the superior turbinate, as well as the roof of the nose. Olfaction has many roles, including recognition of danger and identification of edible food, and is responsible for a significant proportion of the human sense of taste; taste buds on the tongue can only differentiate among sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami (savory) flavors, but the human sense of smell can differentiate as many as 1 trillion odorants. [18] The chorda tympani branch of the facial nerve and the glossopharyngeal nerve provide the oral and pharyngeal portion of the taste pathway, but the major contribution of the sense of smell explains why olfactory decline tends to have a deleterious effect on gustation as well. Of note, the path of the olfactory nerve fibers through the skull base may represent a direct route by which infectious pathogens, such as SARS-CoV-2, may access the central nervous system. [19] The very thin fibers of the olfactory projections that travel through the perforations of the cribriform plate are also highly susceptible to shearing injury, which is why craniofacial trauma may result in hyposmia or anosmia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 1 (Olfactory) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The ventral surface of the olfactory bulb lies on top of the posterior third of the cribriform plate, while the dorsal surface sits beneath the inferior surface of the frontal lobes, specifically the orbital and rectus gyri. The olfactory bulbs serve as a relay station for all impulses transmitted between the olfactory epithelium and the primary olfactory cortex.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 1 (Olfactory) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The olfactory nerve is the first cranial nerve and is instrumental in our sense of smell. The olfactory nerve contains only afferent sensory nerve fibers and, like all cranial nerves, is paired. The olfactory nerve is the shortest cranial nerve, and along with the optic nerve is one of the only two cranial nerves that do not converge with the brainstem. Embryologically, the olfactory nerve is a derivative of the forebrain and is therefore considered a component of the central nervous system. The olfactory nerve is not myelinated by Schwann cells but rather is ensheathed by olfactory ensheathing glia. The olfactory nerves originate from the cell bodies of bipolar olfactory neurons in the olfactory epithelium, a specialized epithelial tissue found in the posterosuperior portion of each nasal cavity. Olfactory neurons give off projections apically towards the outside world and basally towards the olfactory bulb, the central hub, and coordinator of olfactory transmission. From the olfactory bulbs, olfactory information reaches the primary olfactory cortex via the olfactory tract. The primary olfactory cortex interacts with a variety of cortical and limbic structures via sophisticated pathways that allow the smell to become integrated with memory, emotions, and taste. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Ciclesonide and flunisolide are both classified as corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use, meaning they belong to the group of nasal corticosteroid medications applied locally to the nasal mucosa. As members of this therapeutic class, both agents are used to deliver anti‑inflammatory effects directly to the nasal passages, helping to manage conditions such as allergic rhinitis and other inflammatory nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2622
retrieved_docs.content: NASAL PREPARATIONS represent a broad class of pharmaceutical formulations intended for administration via the nasal route, and within this class the specific subgroup of nasal decongestants for systemic use is included. Nasal decongestants for systemic use are a particular type of NASAL PREPARATIONS that are formulated to relieve nasal congestion while also exerting systemic pharmacologic effects, highlighting the diversity of therapeutic options encompassed by nasal drug delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Beclomethasone, fluticasone and Momesalic are all members of the class of corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use. As intranasal glucocorticoids, they act on the nasal mucosa to suppress inflammatory mediators, reduce edema and relieve symptoms of conditions such as allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis and other inflammatory nasal disorders. Because they are applied locally rather than taken systemically, these agents provide effective anti‑inflammatory therapy with a lower risk of systemic side‑effects compared with oral corticosteroids, while sharing a common mechanism of glucocorticoid receptor activation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
retrieved_docs.content: Luxfen is classified as a member of the sympathomimetic agents used as decongestants; these drugs stimulate sympathetic receptors to produce vasoconstriction in the nasal mucosa, thereby reducing swelling and improving airflow in patients with nasal congestion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin is classified as a member of the sympathomimetics used as decongestants, a drug class that exerts its therapeutic effect by stimulating adrenergic receptors to produce vasoconstriction in the nasal mucosa. This vasoconstriction reduces edema and congestion, thereby improving airway patency. Recognizing fenylefrin’s placement within this sympathomimetic decongestant group guides clinicians in its appropriate use for relieving nasal congestion and alerts them to potential adverse effects, such as rebound congestion with prolonged or excessive application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 345
retrieved_docs.content: Increased Protein Synthesis is identified as a physiologic effect of both mupirocin and nusinersen, indicating that despite belonging to different therapeutic classes, these agents share the ability to stimulate protein production. Mupirocin is categorized within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification under the group “Other nasal preparations,” linking it to a specific formulation category for nasal use. Recognizing that mupirocin’s nasal preparation classification and its protein‑synthesis effect, together with the similar effect observed for nusinersen, can inform clinicians about potential mechanistic overlaps and guide monitoring of related outcomes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1173
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with اِضطِراب أنفي, indicating that these interventions address nasal disorders. Within this broader category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of procedure (inverse_isa) that falls under Procedures on Nose. The control of epistaxis procedure is performed directly on the nos, meaning it targets the nose itself. Together, these relationships illustrate that managing nosebleeds is a direct, targeted intervention on the nose and forms part of the wider set of procedures used to treat nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Other nasal preparations in ATC are classified as a type of decongestants and other nasal preparations for topical use, indicating that they belong to the broader category of nasal preparations intended for topical application. NASAL PREPARATIONS, as a superordinate class, encompass decongestants and other nasal preparations for topical use as well as nasal decongestants for systemic use, reflecting the hierarchical organization of nasal drug therapies. Within this classification, mupirocin is recognized as a member of the Other nasal preparations in ATC group, linking the antibiotic to the topical nasal preparation category. Pharmacologically, mupirocin produces the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, highlighting its impact on cellular processes when applied nasally. This integrated view clarifies how specific agents such as mupirocin fit into the ATC nasal preparation taxonomy and underscores the relevance of their mechanistic effects for patient care.
